html:
V

I

вАЬSebastian!вАЭ

Joe started up in bed then fell back weakly. She stared unbelievingly. Sebastian, big, fur-coated, calm and omniscient, smiled placidly down at her.

There was no sign in his face of the sudden pang her appearance had given him. JoeвБ†вАФpoor little Joe!

Her hair had grownвБ†вАФit was arranged in two short plaits, one over each shoulder. Her face was horribly thin, with a high hectic flush on each cheekbone. The bones of her shoulders showed through her thin nightdress.

She looked like a feverish child. There was something childlike in her surprise, in her pleasure, in her eager questioning. The nurse had left them.

Sebastian sat down by the bed and took JoeвАЩs thin hand in his.

вАЬVernon wired me. I didnвАЩt wait for him. I caught the first boat.вАЭ

вАЬTo come to me?вАЭ

вАЬOf course.вАЭ

вАЬDear Sebastian!вАЭ

Tears came into her eyes. Sebastian was alarmed and went on hastily:

вАЬNot that I shanвАЩt do a bit of business while I am over. I often come over on business, and as a matter of fact I can do one or two good deals just now.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt spoil it.вАЭ

вАЬBut itвАЩs true,вАЭ said Sebastian, surprised.

Joe began to laugh, but coughed instead. Sebastian watched anxiously, ready to call the nurse. He had been warned. But the fit passed.

Joe lay there contentedly, her hand creeping into SebastianвАЩs again.

вАЬMother died this way,вАЭ she whispered. вАЬPoor Mother! I thought I was going to be so much wiser than she was, and IвАЩve made such a mess of things. Oh! such a mess of things.вАЭ

вАЬPoor old Joe!вАЭ

вАЬYou donвАЩt know what a mess IвАЩve made of things, Sebastian.вАЭ

вАЬI can imagine it,вАЭ said Sebastian. вАЬI always thought you would.вАЭ

Joe was silent a minute, then she said:

вАЬYou donвАЩt know what a comfort it is to see you, Sebastian. I have seen and known so many rotters. I didnвАЩt like your being strong and successful and cocksureвБ†вАФit annoyed me. But nowвБ†вАФoh, itвАЩs wonderful!вАЭ

He squeezed her hand.

вАЬThereвАЩs no one else in the world who would have comeвБ†вАФas youвАЩve comeвБ†вАФmilesвБ†вАФat once. Vernon, of course, but then heвАЩs a relationвБ†вАФa kind of brother. But youвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm just as much a brotherвБ†вАФmore than a brother. Ever since Abbots Puissants IвАЩve beenвБ†вАФwell, ready to stand by if you wanted me.вАЭ

вАЬOh! Sebastian.вАЭ Her eyes opened wideвБ†вАФhappily. вАЬI never dreamt that youвАЩd feel like that still.вАЭ

He started ever so slightly. He hadnвАЩt meant that exactly. He had meant something that he couldnвАЩt explainвБ†вАФnot at any rate to Joe. It was a feeling peculiarly and exclusively Jewish. The undying gratitude of the Jew who never forgets a benefit conferred. As a child he had been an outcast and Joe had stood by himвБ†вАФshe had been willing to defy her world. The child Sebastian had never forgottenвБ†вАФwould never forget. He would, as he had said, have gone to the ends of the earth if she had wanted him.

She went on.

вАЬThey moved me into this placeвБ†вАФfrom that horrible ward. Was that you?вАЭ

He nodded.

вАЬI cabled.вАЭ

Joe sighed.

вАЬYouвАЩre so terribly efficient, Sebastian.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm afraid so.вАЭ

вАЬBut thereвАЩs nobody like youвБ†вАФnobody. IвАЩve thought of you so often lately.вАЭ

вАЬHave you?вАЭ

He thought of the lonely yearsвБ†вАФthe aching longingвБ†вАФthe baffled desire. Why did things always come to you at the wrong time?

She went on.

вАЬI never dreamt youвАЩd still think about me. I always fancied that some day you and JaneвБ†вАФвАЭ

A queer pang shot through him. JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He and JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He said gruffly: вАЬJane, to my mind, is one of the finest things God ever made. But she belongs body and soul to Vernon and always will.вАЭ

вАЬI suppose so. But itвАЩs a pity. You and she are the strong ones. You belong together.вАЭ

They did, in a curious way. He knew what she meant.

Joe said with a flickering smile: вАЬThis reminds me of the books one reads as a child. Edifying deathbed scenes. Friends and relations gathering round. Wan smiles of heroine.вАЭ

Sebastian had made up his mind. Why had he felt this wasnвАЩt love? It was. This passion of pure disinterested pity and tendernessвБ†вАФthis deep affection lasting through the years. A thousand times better worthwhile than those stormy or tepid affairs that occurred with monotonous regularityвБ†вАФthat punctuated his life without ever touching any real depths.

His heart went out to the childish figure. Somehow, heвАЩd bring it off.

He said gently: вАЬThere arenвАЩt going to be any deathbed scenes, Joe. YouвАЩre going to get well and marry me.вАЭ

вАЬDarling Sebastian! Tie you to a consumptive wife? Of course not.вАЭ

вАЬNonsense! YouвАЩll do one of two thingsвБ†вАФeither get well or die. If you die you die, and thereвАЩs an end of it. If you get cured you marry me. And no expense will be spared to cure you.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm pretty bad, Sebastian dear.вАЭ

вАЬPossibly. But nothing is more uncertain than tubercleвБ†вАФany doctor will tell you so. YouвАЩve been just letting yourself go. I think myself youвАЩll get well. A long weary business, but it can be done.вАЭ

She looked at him. He saw the colour rising and falling in her thin cheeks. He knew then that she loved himвБ†вАФand a queer little stir of warmth woke round his heart. His mother had died two years ago. Since then no one had really cared.

Joe said in a low voice: вАЬSebastian, do you really need me? IвБ†вАФIвАЩve made such a mess of things.вАЭ

He said with sincerity: вАЬNeed you? IвАЩm the loneliest man on earth.вАЭ

And suddenly he broke down. It was a thing he had never done in his lifeвБ†вАФnever thought he would do. He knelt by JoeвАЩs bed, his face buried, his shoulders heaving.

Her hand stroked his head. He knew she was happyвБ†вАФher proud spirit appeased. Dear JoeвБ†вАФso impulsive, so warmhearted, so wrongheaded. She was dearer to him than anyone on earth. They could help one another.

The nurse came inвБ†вАФthe visitor had been there long enough. She withdrew again for Sebastian to say goodbye.

вАЬBy the way,вАЭ he said. вАЬThat French fellowвБ†вАФwhatвАЩs his name?вАЭ

вАЬFran√Іois? HeвАЩs dead.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs all right. You could have got a divorce, of course. But being a widow makes it easier.вАЭ

вАЬYou do think I shall get well?вАЭ

Pathetic, the way she said that!

вАЬOf course.вАЭ

The nurse reappeared and he took his departure. He called on the doctorвБ†вАФhad a long talk. The doctor was not hopeful. But he agreed that there was a chance. They decided on Florida.

Sebastian left the home. He walked along the street deep in thought. He saw a placard with вАЬTerrible Disaster to ResplendentвАЭ on it, but it conveyed nothing to his mind.

He was too busy with his own thoughts. What was really best for Joe? To live or to die? He wondered.

SheвАЩd had such a rotten life. He wanted the best for her.

He went to bed and slept heavily.

II

He awoke to a vague uneasiness. There was somethingвБ†вАФsomething. For the life of him he couldnвАЩt put a name to it.

It wasnвАЩt Joe. Joe was in the foreground of his mind. This was something in the backgroundвБ†вАФshoved awayвБ†вАФsomething that he hadnвАЩt been able to give consideration to at the time.

He thought: вАЬI shall remember presently.вАЭ But he didnвАЩt.

As he dressed, he thought out the problem of Joe. He was all for moving her to Florida as soon as possible. Later, perhaps, Switzerland. She was very weak, but not too weak to be moved. As soon as she had seen Vernon and JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

They were arrivingвБ†вАФwhen? The Resplendent, wasnвАЩt it? The ResplendentвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

The razor he was holding dropped from his hand. HeвАЩd got it now! Before his eyes rose the vision of a newspaper placard.

The ResplendentвБ†вАФTerrible Disaster.

Vernon and Jane were on the Resplendent.

He rang furiously. A few minutes later he was scanning the morning newspaper. There were now full details to hand. His eyes scanned them rapidly. The Resplendent had struck an icebergвБ†вАФthe death rollвБ†вАФsurvivors.

A list of namesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ survivors. He found the name there of Groen, Vernon was alive anyway. Then he searched the other list and found at last what he was looking for, fearingвБ†вАФthe name of Jane Harding.

III

He stood quite still, staring at the newssheet in his hand. Presently he folded it up neatly, laid it on a side table and rang the bell. In a few minutes a curt order given to the bellhop sent his secretary hurrying to him.

вАЬIвАЩve got an appointment at ten oвАЩclock I canвАЩt break. There are some things youвАЩve got to find out for me. Have the information ready for me when I return.вАЭ

He detailed the points succinctly. The fullest particulars as to the Resplendent were to be collected, and certain radios were to be sent off.

Sebastian telephoned himself to the hospital and warned them that no mention of the Resplendent disaster was to be made to the patient. He had a few words with Joe herself which he managed to make normal and commonplace.

He stopped at a florist to send her some flowers and then went off to embark on a long day of meetings and business appointments. It is to be doubted if anyone noticed that the great Sebastian Levinne was unlike himself in the smallest detail. He had never been more shrewd in driving a bargain and his power of getting his own way was never more in evidence.

It was six oвАЩclock when he returned to the Biltmore.

His secretary met him with all the information available. The survivors had been picked up by a Norwegian ship. They would be due in New York in three daysвАЩ time.

Sebastian nodded, his face unchanged. He gave further instructions.

On the evening of the third day following that, he returned to his hotel to be met by the information that Mr.¬†Groen had arrived and was installed in the suite adjoining his own.

Sebastian strode there.

Vernon was standing by the window. He turned round. Sebastian felt something like a shock. In some strange way, he no longer recognized his friend. Something had happened to him.

They stood staring at each other. Sebastian spoke first. He said the thing that all day had been present in his mind.

вАЬJaneвАЩs dead,вАЭ he said.

Vernon noddedвБ†вАФgravelyвБ†вАФunderstandingly.

вАЬYes,вАЭ he said quietly. вАЬJaneвАЩs deadвБ†вАФand I killed her.вАЭ

The old unemotional Sebastian revived and protested.

вАЬFor GodвАЩs sake, Vernon, donвАЩt take it like that. She came with youвБ†вАФnaturally. DonвАЩt be morbid about it.вАЭ

вАЬYou donвАЩt understand,вАЭ said Vernon. вАЬYou donвАЩt know what happened.вАЭ

He paused and then went on, speaking very quietly and collectedly.

вАЬI canвАЩt describe the thing. It happened quite suddenly, you knowвБ†вАФin the middle of the night. There was very little time. The boat heeled over, you know, at an appalling angle. The two of them came togetherвБ†вАФslippingвБ†вАФsliding down the deck. They couldnвАЩt save themselves.вАЭ

вАЬWhat two?вАЭ

вАЬNell and Jane, of course.вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs Nell got to do with it?вАЭ

вАЬShe was on board.вАЭ

вАЬWhat?вАЭ

вАЬYes. I didnвАЩt know. Jane and I were second-class, of course, and I donвАЩt think we ever glanced at a passenger list. Yes, Nell and George Chetwynd were on board. ThatвАЩs what IвАЩm telling you, if you wouldnвАЩt interrupt. It happenedвБ†вАФa sort of nightmareвБ†вАФno time for life boats or anything. I was hanging on to a stanchionвБ†вАФor whatever you call itвБ†вАФto save myself from falling into the sea.

вАЬAnd they came drifting along the deck, those two, right by meвБ†вАФslipping, slidingвБ†вАФfaster and fasterвБ†вАФand the sea waiting for them below.

вАЬIвАЩd no idea Nell was on board till I saw her drifting down to destruction, and crying out, вАШVernon!вАЩ

вАЬThere isnвАЩt time to think on these occasions, I tell you. One can just make an instinctive gesture. I could grab on to one or other of themвБ†вАФNell or Jane. I grabbed Nell and held her, held her like grim death.вАЭ

вАЬAnd Jane?вАЭ

Vernon said quietly:

вАЬI can see her face still, looking at me as she wentвБ†вАФdown into that green swirlвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬMy God!вАЭ said Sebastian hoarsely.

Then suddenly his impassivity forsook him. His voice rang out bellowing like a bull.

вАЬYou saved Nell? You bloody fool! To save NellвБ†вАФand let Jane drown. Why, Nell isnвАЩt worth the tip of JaneвАЩs little finger. Damn you!вАЭ

вАЬI know that.вАЭ

вАЬYou know it? ThenвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬI tell you, it isnвАЩt what you knowвБ†вАФitвАЩs some blind instinct that takes hold of you.вАЭ

вАЬDamn you! Damn you!вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm damned all right. You neednвАЩt worry. I let Jane drownвБ†вАФand I love her.вАЭ

вАЬLove her?вАЭ

вАЬYes, IвАЩve always loved her. I see that now. Always, from the beginning, I was afraid of herвБ†вАФbecause I loved her. I was a coward there, like everywhere elseвБ†вАФtrying to escape from reality. I fought against her. I was ashamed of the power she had over me. IвАЩve taken her through hell.

вАЬAnd now I want herвБ†вАФI want her. Oh! youвАЩll say thatвАЩs like me, to want a thing as soon as itвАЩs out of my reach. Perhaps itвАЩs trueвБ†вАФperhaps I am like that.

вАЬI only know that I love JaneвБ†вАФthat I love herвБ†вАФand that sheвАЩs gone from me forever.вАЭ

He sat down on a chair and said in his normal tone: вАЬI want to work. Get out of here, Sebastian, thereвАЩs a good fellow.вАЭ

вАЬMy God, Vernon, I didnвАЩt think I could ever hate youвБ†вАФвАЭ

Vernon repeated: вАЬI want to work.вАЭ

Sebastian turned on his heel and left the room.

IV

Vernon sat very still.

JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Horrible to suffer like thisвБ†вАФto want anyone so much.

JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Yes, heвАЩd always loved her. After that very first meeting heвАЩd been unable to keep away. HeвАЩd been drawn towards her by something stronger than himself.

Fool and coward to be afraidвБ†вАФalways afraid. Afraid of any deep realityвБ†вАФof any violent emotion.

And she had knownвБ†вАФshe had always known, and been unable to help him. What had she said: вАЬDivided in timeвАЭ? That first evening at SebastianвАЩs party when she had sung:

вАЬI saw a Fairy lady there
With long white hands and drowning hair.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Drowning hairвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ no, no, not that. Queer she should have sung that song. And the statue of the drowned womanвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That was queer, too.

What was the other thing she had sung that night?

вАЬJвАЩai perdu mon amieвБ†вАФelle est morte.
Tout sвАЩen va cette fois, √† jamais,
√А jamais, pour toujours elle emporte
Le dernier des amours que jвАЩaimais.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

He had lost Abbots Puissants, he had lost Nell.

But with Jane, he had indeed lost вАЬle dernier des amours que jвАЩaimais.вАЭ

For the rest of his life he would be able to see only one womanвБ†вАФJane.

He loved JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ he loved her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

And heвАЩd tortured her, slighted her, finally abandoned her to that green evil sea.

The statue in the South Kensington MuseumвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

GodвБ†вАФhe mustnвАЩt think of that.

YesвБ†вАФheвАЩd think of everything. This time he wouldnвАЩt turn away.

JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He wanted her. JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

HeвАЩd never see her again.

HeвАЩd lost everything nowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ everything.

Those days, months, years in Russia. Wasted years.

FoolвБ†вАФto live beside her, to hold her body in his arms, and all the time to be afraidвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Afraid of his passion for her.

That old terror of the BeastвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

And suddenly, as he thought of the Beast, he knewвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Knew that at last he had come into his heritage.

V

It was like the day he had come back from the Titanic Concert. It was the vision he had had then. He called it vision for it seemed more that than sound. Seeing and hearing were oneвБ†вАФcurves and spirals of soundвБ†вАФascending, descending, returning.

And now he knewвБ†вАФhe had the technical knowledge.

He snatched at paper, jotted down brief, scrawled hieroglyphics, a kind of frantic shorthand. There were years of work in front of him, but he knew that he should never again recapture this first freshness and clearness of vision.

It must be soвБ†вАФand so: a whole weight of metalвБ†вАФbrassвБ†вАФall the brass in the world.

And those new glass soundsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ringing, clear.

He was happy.

An hour passedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ two hours.

For a moment he came out of his frenzyвБ†вАФrememberedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Jane!

He felt sickвБ†вАФashamed. CouldnвАЩt he even mourn her for one evening? There was something baseвБ†вАФcruelвБ†вАФin the way he was using his sorrow, his desireвБ†вАФtransmuting it into terms of sound.

That was what it meant being a creatorвБ†вАФruthlessness, using everything.

And people like Jane were the victims.

JaneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He felt torn in twoвБ†вАФagony and wild exultation.

He thought: вАЬPerhaps women feel like this when they have a child.вАЭ

Presently he bent again over his sheets of paper, writing frenziedly, flinging them on the floor as he finished them.

When the door opened he did not hear it. He was deaf to the rustle of a womanвАЩs dress. Only when a small frightened voice said, вАЬVernon,вАЭ did he look up.

With an effort he forced the abstracted look from his face.

вАЬHullo,вАЭ he said. вАЬNell.вАЭ

She stood there, twisting her hands togetherвБ†вАФher face white and ravaged. She spoke in breathless gasps.

вАЬVernonвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I found out. They told meвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ where you wereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and I came.вАЭ

He nodded.

вАЬYes,вАЭ he said. вАЬYou came?вАЭ

OboesвБ†вАФno, cut out oboes. Too soft a note. It must be stridentвБ†вАФbrazen. But harpsвБ†вАФyes, he wanted the liquidness of harpsвБ†вАФlike water. You wanted water as a source of power.

Bother! Nell was speaking. HeвАЩd have to listen.

вАЬVernon, after that awful escape from deathвБ†вАФI knew.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ThereвАЩs only one thing that mattersвБ†вАФlove. IвАЩve always loved you. IвАЩve come back to youвБ†вАФfor always.вАЭ

вАЬOh!вАЭ he said stupidly.

She had come nearer, was holding out her hands to him.

He looked at her as if from a great distance. Really, Nell was extraordinarily pretty. He could well see why he had fallen in love with her. Queer, that he wasnвАЩt the least bit in love with her now. How awkward it all was. He did wish she would go away and let him get on with what he was doing. What about trombones? One could improve on a tromboneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬVernon!вАЭ Her voice was sharpвБ†вАФfrightened. вАЬDonвАЩt you love me any more?вАЭ

It was really best to be truthful. He said with an odd formal politeness:

вАЬIвАЩm awfully sorry. IвБ†вАФIвАЩm afraid I donвАЩt. You see, I love Jane.вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩre angry with meвБ†вАФbecause of that lie about theвБ†вАФthe child.вАЭ

вАЬWhat lie? About what child?вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt you even remember? I said I was going to have a child and it wasnвАЩt true.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh! Vernon, forgive meвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ forgive me.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs quite all right, Nell. DonвАЩt you worry. IвАЩm sure everythingвАЩs for the best. George is an awfully good chap and youвАЩre really happiest with him. And now, for GodвАЩs sake, do go away. I donвАЩt want to be rude, but IвАЩm most awfully busy. The whole thing will go if I donвАЩt pin it down.вАЭ

She stared at him.

Then slowly she moved towards the door. She stopped, turned, flung out her hands towards him.

вАЬVernonвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

It was a last cry of despairing appeal.

He did not even look up, only shook his head impatiently.

She went out, shutting the door behind her.

Vernon gave a sigh of relief.

There was nothing now to come between him and his workвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

He bent over the table.
